These angiograms show the patent lima LAD and the diseased vein graft.
The vein was harvested in the usual fashion and loaded onto a cardiac passport and asthmatic device.
A left thoracotomy was performed with the patient in the full lateral position to expose the descending thoracic aorta and the heart.
No displacement of the heart is necessary to access the lateral wall.
The aorta had been examined by TEE and shown to be free of disease.
A suitable spot was selected.
The lung had been retracted superiorly and the device was fired.
A vascular right angle is used to help the extraction of the vein graft from the device.
Flow is checked and found to be excellent in the vein graft.
The vein is marked to help with the orientation, and the target vessel is selected.
In this instance, the diseased portion of the vein graft was located somewhat proximal to the anastomosis to the native coronary artery, which itself was noted to be widely patent.

I opted here to perform the anastomosis to the most distal portion of the previously placed saphenous vein graft as this was quite soft and disease free with a widely patent anastomosis to the obtuse marginal artery.
Easy access is obtained to the native coronary artery should one desire to place a bypass there.
The vein graft is born inferior to the pulmonary hilum, and here you see one is experimenting with the orientation of the vein graft.
A venotomy is made in the most distal portion of the vein graft and extended appropriately.
A standard hand sewn anastomosis is now performed using a running 7-0 proline suture.
As mentioned previously, the newly placed vein graft has been brought inferior to the pulmonary hilum.
In this instance, I chose this configuration where the vane takes a gentle U. Minimal displacement of the heart was required as seen previously.
Transit time flow measurements were now performed as is our customary practice.
And as one can see, the flows in this particular instance were excellent.
The lung was reinflated and standard closure was performed of this left thoracotomy.
